section.title: Ruth Gleans in Boaz's Field
section.sectionRationale: The second act is a single day in a barley field that changes everything. Ruth goes out to glean and happens onto the field of Boaz, a worthy kinsman, who shows her extraordinary favor and protection. Naomi recognizes God's hand and names Boaz as one of their kinsman-redeemers. It is one movement introducing the redeemer and turning Naomi's bitterness toward hope.
section.thesis: As a poor foreign widow gleans for survival, the hidden providence of God guides her to the field of a worthy redeemer whose lavish kindness begins to fill the empty and reveals that the LORD has not withdrawn His loving devotion from the living or the dead.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Ruth sets out to glean and happens onto Boaz's field; Boaz notices and blesses her; his lavish provision at mealtime; and Naomi's discovery that Boaz is a kinsman-redeemer.

units.label: Ruth happens onto Boaz's field
units.note: the providence of a chance meeting

verses.text: Now Naomi had a relative on her husband’s side, a prominent man of {{chayil|noble character}} from the clan of Elimelech, whose name was Boaz.
And Ruth the Moabitess said to Naomi, “Please let me go into the fields and glean heads of grain after someone in whose sight I may find {{chen|favor}}.” “Go ahead, my daughter,” Naomi replied.
So Ruth departed and went out into the field and {{laqat|gleaned}} after the harvesters. And she happened to come to the part of the field belonging to Boaz, who was from the clan of Elimelech.

units.label: Boaz notices and blesses Ruth
units.note: under whose wings you have taken refuge

verses.text: Just then Boaz arrived from Bethlehem and said to the harvesters, “The LORD be with you.” “The LORD bless you,” they replied.
And Boaz asked the foreman of his harvesters, “Whose young woman is this?”
The foreman answered, “She is the Moabitess who returned with Naomi from the land of Moab.
She has said, ‘Please let me glean and gather among the sheaves after the harvesters.’ So she came out and has continued from morning until now, except that she rested a short time in the shelter.”
Then Boaz said to Ruth, “Listen, my daughter. Do not go and glean in another field, and do not go away from this place, but stay here close to my servant girls.

Let your eyes be on the field they are harvesting, and follow along after these girls. Indeed, I have ordered the young men not to touch you. And when you are thirsty, go and drink from the jars the young men have filled.”
At this, she fell on her face, bowing low to the ground, and said to him, “Why have I found such favor in your eyes that you should take notice of me, even though I am a foreigner?”

Boaz replied, “I have been made fully aware of all you have done for your mother-in-law since the death of your husband, how you left your father and mother and the land of your birth, and how you came to a people you did not know before.
May the LORD repay your work, and may you receive a rich reward from the LORD, the God of Israel, under whose {{kanaph|wings}} you have taken refuge.”
“My lord,” she said, “may I continue to find favor in your eyes, for you have comforted and spoken kindly to your maidservant, though I am not like one of your servant girls.”

units.label: Lavish provision at the meal
units.note: she ate and was satisfied and had some left over

verses.text: At mealtime Boaz said to her, “Come over here; have some bread and dip it into the vinegar sauce.” So she sat down beside the harvesters, and he offered her roasted grain, and she ate and was satisfied and had some left over.
When Ruth got up to glean, Boaz ordered his young men, “Even if she gathers among the sheaves, do not insult her.
Rather, pull out for her some stalks from the bundles and leave them for her to gather. Do not rebuke her.”
So Ruth gathered grain in the field until evening. And when she beat out what she had gleaned, it was about an ephah of barley.

units.label: Naomi recognizes a kinsman-redeemer
units.note: the LORD has not withdrawn His kindness

verses.text: She picked up the grain and went into the town, where her mother-in-law saw what she had gleaned. And she brought out what she had saved from her meal and gave it to Naomi.

Then her mother-in-law asked her, “Where did you glean today, and where did you work? Blessed be the man who noticed you.” So she told her mother-in-law where she had worked. “The name of the man I worked with today is Boaz,” she said.

Then Naomi said to her daughter-in-law, “May he be blessed by the LORD, who has not withdrawn His {{chesed|kindness}} from the living or the dead.” Naomi continued, “The man is a close relative. He is one of our {{goel|kinsman-redeemers}}.”
Then Ruth the Moabitess said, “He also told me, ‘Stay with my young men until they have finished gathering all my harvest.’”
And Naomi said to her daughter-in-law Ruth, “My daughter, it is good for you to work with his young women, so that nothing will happen to you in another field.”
So Ruth stayed close to the servant girls of Boaz to glean grain until the barley and wheat harvests were finished. And she lived with her mother-in-law.

chayil.gloss: Strength, worth, valor, or noble character. Boaz is introduced as a gibbor chayil, a man of standing and moral weight, not merely wealth. The same phrase, eshet chayil, crowns Ruth in 3:11 and the virtuous woman of Proverbs 31:10; the book deliberately pairs a worthy man with a worthy woman whom God brings together.

chen.gloss: Favor, grace, undeserved kindness. Ruth hopes to glean behind someone in whose sight she may find favor, and marvels that she, a foreigner, has found it. The word quietly points to grace, kindness shown to one who has no claim on it, which is exactly how Boaz treats her and how God treats sinners.

laqat.gloss: To glean, gather, or pick up. The law commanded landowners to leave the edges of their fields and any dropped grain for the poor, the widow, and the foreigner to gather, Leviticus 19:9-10 and Deuteronomy 24:19-21. Ruth is precisely the person the gleaning laws were meant to protect, and Boaz obeys the law with overflowing generosity.

kanaph.gloss: Wing, edge, or corner of a garment. Boaz blesses Ruth for taking refuge under the wings of the God of Israel. In 3:9 Ruth will ask Boaz to spread the corner of his garment, the same word, over her; what Boaz prays that God will do, God answers through Boaz himself, who becomes the very refuge he blessed.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, steadfast love, loyal covenant kindness. Naomi sees Boaz's generosity as evidence that the LORD has not withdrawn His chesed from the living or the dead. It is the great theme of Ruth, and here it turns Naomi's bitterness toward hope as she recognizes God's faithful hand.

goel.gloss: Kinsman-redeemer or guardian-redeemer, a near relative legally responsible to rescue a family member in need: to buy back sold land, redeem a relative from slavery, and raise up a name for the dead. The BSB footnote points to Leviticus 25:25-55. Naomi's naming of Boaz as one of their redeemers is the hinge of the plot and a shadow of Christ, our Redeemer.

basic.context: To keep herself and Naomi alive, Ruth goes out to glean, gathering leftover grain that the law reserved for the poor. By what looks like chance she ends up in a field owned by Boaz, a well-off and godly relative of her dead father-in-law. Boaz has heard of her loyalty to Naomi, and he protects her, feeds her, and orders his men to leave extra grain for her. When Ruth tells Naomi, Naomi realizes Boaz is a kinsman-redeemer, and her hope begins to revive.
basic.hermeneutics: This is narrative packed with quiet theology. The word happened in verse 3 is deliberate understatement: what looks like luck is really God's providence. Boaz is held up as a model of generosity and integrity, and Naomi's response teaches us to read good gifts as evidence of God's steadfast love. The gleaning laws show God's heart for the poor and the outsider.
basic.originalLanguages: Boaz is a man of chayil, worth and noble character; Ruth hopes for chen, favor or grace, and keeps finding it. She comes under the kanaph, the wings, of God for refuge, the same word she will use of Boaz's garment in chapter three. Naomi sees God's chesed, loving devotion, at work and names Boaz a goel, a kinsman-redeemer.
basic.typology: Boaz the kinsman-redeemer is the clearest picture in the book of Christ our Redeemer: a worthy relative who has the right, the means, and the willing heart to rescue the helpless. His welcome of a foreign widow and his covering of her point to the way Christ receives sinners and outsiders under His wings.
basic.christ: Boaz foreshadows Jesus, the true and better kinsman-redeemer, who takes on our flesh to become our near relative, pays the full price, and rescues the poor and the outsider. Ruth taking refuge under God's wings is answered when Christ gathers His people as a hen gathers her chicks, and the abundant grain that satisfies her points to the fullness we find in Him.
basic.god: God's providence quietly governs the whole day, guiding Ruth to the right field at the right time. We learn that God cares for the poor, the widow, and the foreigner, that He works through ordinary means and generous people, and that His loving devotion has not been withdrawn even when His people feel empty.
basic.crossReferences: Leviticus 19 and Deuteronomy 24 command the gleaning that provides for Ruth. Psalm 91 speaks of refuge under God's wings. And Boaz as redeemer looks ahead to Christ the Redeemer in Titus 2 and 1 Peter 1.
basic.oneStory: The story of redemption advances as God guides an empty widow to a worthy redeemer. Boaz's kindness begins to reverse Naomi's emptiness and introduces the office of kinsman-redeemer that the whole book, and finally the gospel, is about. It all points to Jesus, who redeems the poor and the outsider and covers them with His love.

context.literary: The central act of the book, a self-contained day-in-the-field that introduces Boaz and the redeemer theme. It is framed by conversations with Naomi at the start and end, marking Ruth's movement from empty-handed to laden with grain. The narrator's word happened in verse 3 is a masterstroke of irony, signaling that nothing here is truly by chance.
context.historical: Gleaning was Israel's welfare system: landowners left the field edges and dropped grain for the poor, the widow, the orphan, and the foreigner. A young widow, and a Moabite at that, gleaning among male harvesters was vulnerable to insult and worse, which is why Boaz's orders for her protection matter so much. An ephah of barley was a striking haul, perhaps enough to feed two women for many days.
context.canonical: The office of kinsman-redeemer, rooted in Leviticus 25 and Deuteronomy 25, comes to life in Boaz and becomes one of Scripture's great pictures of redemption. Ruth's inclusion and Boaz's godliness carry the narrative toward the marriage and the child who will secure the line of David and, in time, the Messiah.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To introduce Boaz as a worthy kinsman-redeemer, to display God's hidden providence and His care for the poor and the outsider, and to turn Naomi's bitterness toward hope through the first fruits of redemption.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Boaz's generosity and Ruth's diligence; while it does not legislate, it plainly holds them up as models. The gleaning laws behind the scene are prescriptive, revealing God's abiding concern that the poor and the foreigner be provided for and protected.

debates.question: How should we read the word happened in verse 3, that Ruth chanced onto Boaz's field?

views.label: Deliberate irony pointing to providence
views.case: The narrator uses the language of chance precisely to make the reader smile and look higher: there are no accidents in God's ordering of events, and the whole book insists that the LORD is quietly steering everything toward redemption.

views.label: Ordinary means under God's rule
views.case: Ruth made a free, sensible choice to glean, and God worked through that ordinary decision; the point is not that Ruth was passive but that God governs even our everyday steps.

debates.bearing: Both readings land on the same truth: what looks like coincidence is the fingerprint of God's providence, which does not cancel human action but works through it.

debates.question: What makes Boaz a model of godly character here?

views.label: Covenant-shaped generosity
views.case: Boaz goes far beyond the letter of the gleaning law, ordering extra grain left for Ruth, feeding her at his table, and guarding her honor, embodying the chesed the law was meant to produce.

views.label: Faith expressed in blessing and protection
views.case: His speech is saturated with the LORD, he blesses Ruth in God's name, and he protects the vulnerable outsider, showing a faith that works itself out in tangible kindness.

debates.bearing: Either emphasis presents Boaz as a picture of righteous love that anticipates Christ, the Redeemer whose generosity and protection know no limit.

study.originalLanguages: The scene is threaded with key words. Boaz is a gibbor chayil, a man of worth and noble character, the masculine of the very phrase, eshet chayil, that will crown Ruth in 3:11 and the woman of Proverbs 31. Ruth sets out hoping to find chen, favor or grace, in someone's eyes, and the word recurs as she marvels that a foreigner should be so favored. She has come to take refuge under the kanaph, the wings, of the God of Israel, a word that returns in 3:9 when she asks Boaz to spread the corner of his garment, the same term, over her. Naomi reads Boaz's kindness as proof that the LORD has not withdrawn His chesed, loving devotion, and names Boaz a goel, a kinsman-redeemer, the office rooted in Leviticus 25 that drives the rest of the story.

typology.fulfillment: Christ, our near kinsman who takes our flesh and has the right, the means, and the will to redeem the helpless, Hebrews 2:14-17.

typology.type: Ruth taking refuge under the wings of the LORD
typology.fulfillment: Christ gathering His people under His wings, longing to shelter even outsiders, Matthew 23:37.

typology.type: The abundant grain that satisfies and overflows
typology.fulfillment: The fullness of provision and life found in Christ, who satisfies the hungry and gives more than enough, John 6:35.

study.christ: Boaz is the book's brightest signpost to Jesus. A kinsman-redeemer had to be a near relative, willing and able to pay the price to rescue his own; Christ became our near relative by taking on flesh, Hebrews 2:14-17, and paid the full price to redeem the poor and the outsider. Boaz notices, welcomes, feeds, and shields a foreign widow with no claim on him, a living picture of grace, chen, given to the undeserving. Ruth's refuge under the wings of God is answered ultimately in Christ, who longs to gather His people as a hen gathers her chicks, Matthew 23:37, sheltering even those once far off.
study.god: Though God performs no visible miracle, His providence governs the entire day, guiding Ruth to Boaz's field at the very hour Boaz arrives. We learn that the LORD keeps watch over the poor, the widow, and the stranger, and that He often works not by spectacle but through ordinary decisions and generous people. Above all, Naomi's confession reveals the God whose chesed, loving devotion, has not been withdrawn from the living or the dead; the same God who seemed to empty her is the God quietly filling her hands again.

crossReferences.note: The command to leave the edges and gleanings of the field for the poor and the foreigner, the law that sustains Ruth.
Sheaves and produce left in the field are for the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow.
He will cover you with His feathers, and under His wings you will find refuge, the image Boaz applies to Ruth.
Christ shares in our flesh and blood, becoming our merciful kinsman to make atonement, the reality Boaz foreshadows.

study.oneStory: Redemption's story moves forward as God guides an empty, foreign widow to the field of a worthy redeemer. Boaz's overflowing kindness begins to reverse Naomi's emptiness and brings the office of kinsman-redeemer onto the stage, the very office the whole book, and finally the gospel, is about. Ruth sheltering under the wings of the LORD and filled with abundant grain points straight to Jesus, the true Redeemer, who welcomes the poor and the outsider and satisfies them forever.

sermon.title: Favor to the Humble
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Ruth, this message expounds Ruth 2:4-13, the meeting of Boaz and Ruth in the field, his kindness to the foreign widow, and her refuge under the wings of the God of Israel. A clear, orthodox handling of the heart of this movement.

quiz.q: How did Ruth end up gleaning in the field of Boaz?

options.text: She happened onto his field while gleaning, an ordinary event that the narrator hints was God's providence.
options.note: Ruth 2:3 says she happened to come to Boaz's field, quiet irony for God's guiding hand.

options.text: Naomi arranged in advance for her to work for Boaz.
options.note: Naomi only recognized Boaz afterward; Ruth came to his field before either planned it.

options.text: Boaz sent servants to bring her to his field.
options.note: Ruth came on her own initiative to glean; Boaz noticed her once she was there.

options.text: She was assigned there by the town elders.
options.note: No official assigned her; she chose to glean and providence did the rest.

quiz.q: How did Boaz treat Ruth in his field?

options.text: He protected her, fed her, and ordered extra grain left for her, going beyond the gleaning law.
options.note: Ruth 2:8-16 shows his lavish, protective generosity to the vulnerable widow.

options.text: He allowed her to glean but kept his distance.
options.note: He did far more than tolerate her; he actively provided for and protected her.

options.text: He warned her not to return the next day.
options.note: The opposite: he urged her to stay in his field for the whole harvest.

options.text: He demanded payment for the grain she gathered.
options.note: He gave freely and generously, asking nothing in return.

quiz.q: What did Boaz say Ruth had done in coming to Israel?

options.text: She had taken refuge under the wings of the LORD, the God of Israel.
options.note: Ruth 2:12 blesses her for seeking refuge under God's wings.

options.text: She had abandoned her duty to her family.
options.note: He praised her loyalty to Naomi, not abandonment; the key image is refuge under God's wings.

options.text: She had come merely to find a wealthy husband.
options.note: Boaz commends her faith and loyalty, not any scheme for wealth.

options.text: She had betrayed the gods of Moab for no reason.
options.note: He honors her turning to the true God as taking refuge under His wings.

quiz.q: What did Naomi realize when she heard the name Boaz?

options.text: That Boaz was a close relative and one of their kinsman-redeemers.
options.note: Ruth 2:20 has Naomi recognize Boaz as a goel, a kinsman-redeemer.

options.text: That Boaz was an enemy to be avoided.
options.note: The opposite: she saw him as a redeemer and a sign of God's kindness.

options.text: That Boaz was a stranger with no connection to them.
options.note: He was in fact a close relative from Elimelech's clan.

options.text: That Boaz intended to take their land unjustly.
options.note: Naomi saw him as a redeemer, evidence of God's steadfast love, not a threat.

quiz.q: How does Boaz point us to Jesus?

options.text: As a worthy kinsman-redeemer who has the right, the means, and the will to rescue the helpless.
options.note: Boaz foreshadows Christ, our near kinsman who redeems the poor and the outsider.

options.text: Only as a kind man with no deeper meaning.
options.note: His kindness is real, but Scripture presents the redeemer role as a picture of Christ.

options.text: As a warning against helping the poor.
options.note: He is a model of generosity, not a warning against it.

options.text: As proof that redemption depends on our own effort.
options.note: Redemption comes through the redeemer's initiative and provision, not the widow's effort.

questions: Ruth stepped out in humble, hard work, and God met her there. Where is God calling you to faithful ordinary effort while trusting Him with the outcome?
What looked like chance was really God's providence guiding Ruth to the right field. Where have you later seen God's hand in what felt like coincidence at the time?
Boaz went far beyond duty to protect and provide for a vulnerable outsider. Who around you is vulnerable or overlooked, and how could you show them that kind of generous kindness?
Ruth took refuge under the wings of the God of Israel. What would it look like for you to run to God for shelter this week rather than to lesser things?
Naomi began to see that God had not withdrawn His steadfast love after all. Where do you need to notice God's kindness reviving hope in a season that has felt empty?

translationNotes.note: A prominent man of noble character renders gibbor chayil, worth and standing; the same root, chayil, describes Ruth in 3:11 and the woman of Proverbs 31:10.
She happened to come to Boaz's field is deliberate understatement; the whole book treats such chance as the working of God's providence.
Wings renders kanaph, which can also mean the corner of a garment; Ruth uses the same word of Boaz's garment in 3:9, so the blessing is answered through Boaz himself.
Kinsman-redeemer renders goel, a legal term for the relative who redeems or vindicates his kin; the BSB footnote points to Leviticus 25:25-55.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Ruth 2:1-23. An ephah of barley in verse 17 is roughly 22 liters, about 29 pounds, per the BSB footnote.
